this activity diagram models the flow of an application startup, checking user login status, and handling login and registration requests.
the main flow starts at the initial node "start," checks if the user is logged in, and proceeds to the home page if logged in or to the login/registration fragment if not.
if the user is not logged in, it prompts for login or registration, then checks if the login or registration was successful, leading back to the home page if successful.
key edge cases include handling both successful login and successful registration, ensuring the user reaches the home page upon completion.

this activity diagram models the process of managing proposals, starting from the home page. it checks if data about proposals is available, then shows either received or sent proposals. it further checks if a proposal has been accepted and allows viewing the details of the proposal. the diagram includes decision points to handle different scenarios such as when data is not available or when a proposal is accepted.

this activity diagram models the process of listing and viewing job offers.
the main flow starts at the home page, checks for available offer data, and navigates to either the list of offers or the search filters fragment.
if data is available, it proceeds to the offer list fragment and then to the offer detail activity.
if data is not available, it goes through the search filters fragment before proceeding to the offer list fragment and then to the offer detail activity.
there are two distinct main flows depending on whether data is available or not.

this activity diagram models the process of modifying personal data. the user starts at the home page, navigates to the activity for viewing their profile, then proceeds to the fragment for modifying personal data. after the modification, a decision is made about whether the change was successful. if successful, the process ends; if not, it returns to the fragment for further modifications.

the diagram starts at the home page and navigates through activities related to saved elements.
users can either view their saved offers or remove them.
if removal is successful, it returns to the offer list; otherwise, it stays in the removal fragment.
there are two main paths: viewing saved offers and removing them.

this diagram models the registration process for a user.
the client (utente) provides data and accepts a privacy document.
the actor (user) processes the data and inserts it into the database.
the server (database) responds with either success or failure.
the main flow involves the client sending data, the actor processing it, and the server responding back.

database -> user — type=message; label="response"; explanation="database responds with success or failure"
user -> main — type=message; label="if success go to main page"; explanation="user navigates to main page if login is successful"
flow 1: utente -> user -> database -> user -> main
this diagram models a user login process.
the user inputs their credentials and attempts to sign in.
the application sends the credentials to the database for verification.
upon successful authentication, the user is redirected to the main page.
there are no explicit error paths or conditions shown in the diagram.

this activity diagram models the process of displaying a user's inbox.
the user initiates the process by navigating to the myboardactivity.
the myboardactivity then retrieves the user's items from the database.
the retrieved items are sent back to the myboardactivity, which finally displays the items list to the user.
there are no conditions or branches in this diagram, indicating a straightforward linear flow.

this sequence diagram models the process of inserting an object/service into a bulletin board.
the user initiates the insertion by clicking a button.
the system requests an image, which can be taken or not.
information about the object/service is then requested and inserted into the database.
the database responds, and the updated list of items is displayed to the user.
there is a conditional branch for taking a picture, leading to different paths depending on whether the picture is taken or not.

this sequence diagram models the process of deleting an object/service from a folder.
the user initiates the deletion by clicking a delete button.
a confirmation request is sent to the user.
if confirmed, the item is deleted from the database.
the updated list of items is then displayed to the user.
the diagram captures the essential steps and interactions involved in the deletion process.

this activity diagram models the process of exploring offers.
the user initiates the process by showing a list of items.
the application retrieves items from the database.
the application then responds with the retrieved items.
the user sees the list of items.

database -> exchange — type=message; label="response"; explanation="database responds to the exchange service"
flow 1: utente -> exchange — "propose new exchange" -> exchange -> database — "save exchange info in database" -> database -> exchange — "response" -> exchange -> utente — "show exchange list"
this sequence diagram models the process of a user proposing a new exchange.
the user initiates the process by proposing a new exchange to the exchange service.
the exchange service then saves the information about the proposed exchange into the database.
after saving the information, the exchange service sends a response back to the user.
finally, the exchange service returns the list of exchanges to the user.
there are no conditions or branches in the diagram, indicating a straightforward linear process.

exchange -> database — type=association; label="get exchanges info from database"; explanation="exchange retrieves data from database"
database -> exchange — type=response; label="response"; explanation="database sends response back to exchange"

this activity diagram models the process of a user navigating to an exchange page, retrieving exchange information from a database, and displaying the list of exchanges.

the main flow involves the user initiating the process, which then moves to the exchange service, retrieves data from the database, receives a response, and finally displays the list of exchanges to the user.
there are no conditions or branches in the diagram, indicating a straightforward linear process without decision points.

this activity diagram models the process of accepting or rejecting an exchange proposal.

the main flow involves the user navigating to the exchange page, retrieving and displaying the exchange list, clicking on an accept/reject button, requiring confirmation, confirming the action, updating the exchange status, and finally showing the updated exchange list.
there are no conditions or branches in the diagram.
the process is linear and does not involve any loops or repetitions.

the user can insert a new filter.
the application then retrieves items from the database based on the filter.
finally, the application displays the filtered items to the user.

this sequence diagram models the process of modifying personal data.
the user initiates the process by navigating to their personal information.
the profile retrieves the current data from the database.
the user is then prompted to modify the information.
new information is entered by the user.
the profile saves the new information back to the database.
finally, the updated information is displayed to the user.
there are no branching conditions or loops in this process.
